---
title: "6. Descriptive, Predictive, and Causal Questions"
---

[![Open the lab in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/notebooks/student/nb01_curiosity_to_problem_student.ipynb){target="_blank"}

> **The research decision.** This chapter equips you to classify any research
> question on the **inquiry compass** by its *kind* (descriptive or causal) and its
> *reach* (the data at hand, a population, or cases not yet seen), then defend that
> placement by the answer the question actually wants rather than the words it
> happens to carry.

## Why this decision matters

A thesis advisor at your first committee meeting puts it plainly: *"Before I approve
a single method, I make you answer two questions about your question. What kind of
answer does it want, and for whom must that answer hold?"* Everything downstream sits
inside the box those two answers draw. Misclassify your question and every later
method aims at the wrong target, because a method that describes what is there cannot
tell you what an intervention would change. The compass fixes what your evidence may
claim before you spend a term collecting it.

## The concept

Every project narrows to a **research question**: the single sentence evidence can
answer and be wrong about, such as "does a lid slow a mug's cooling?" Before you
pick a method, you classify that question on the **inquiry compass**, the course map
that sorts any question by exactly two things. The compass is adapted from *Research
Design in the Social Sciences* (Blair, Coppock, and Humphreys, 2023, ch. 7).

The first axis is **kind**. A **descriptive question** asks what the world is or was,
with no intervention needed; you look and record, as in "what fraction of first-years
skip breakfast?" A **causal question** asks what would change if someone intervened,
which forces a comparison to a world that did not happen, as in "does a free
breakfast raise attendance?"

The second axis is **reach**: for which units the answer must hold. **Data at hand**
means only the units you actually observed. **A population** means a larger group
your sampling reaches beyond the data. **Unseen cases** means units you have not
observed yet, usually future ones.

Crossing the two axes gives four named positions. **Description** is descriptive
about the data at hand. **Generalization** is descriptive about a population, and it
must pay for its reach with a sampling design that lets the units you saw stand in for
the ones you did not. **Prediction** is descriptive about unseen cases; it forecasts a
new case from a pattern. Prediction is still descriptive, not causal: guessing who
will drop out is not the same as knowing what makes them drop out. **Causal
reasoning** is the causal kind, the only position that earns the word "because."

One last hazard. A **double-barreled question** is one sentence that secretly asks two
questions of different kinds, such as "how common is burnout, and does night work
cause it?" It cannot be answered as one, and the fix is to split it into two clean
questions, each with its own position.

## A worked example

Start with a physics curiosity: "why does my coffee go cold so fast?" Give it edges
and it becomes a **topic**, heat loss from travel mugs. That one topic hides four
different questions, one at each compass position. Newton's law of cooling, a standard
result in introductory physics, says a warm object loses heat faster the hotter it is
than the room. Against that backdrop you time twelve mugs as each cools from 90 °C to
60 °C.

1. *"In these twelve mugs, what was the average time to cool from 90 to 60 °C?"* This
   asks only what your recorded numbers say. It is **descriptive** with reach **data
   at hand**. Position: **Description**.
2. *"Across every mug of this model ever made, what is the mean cooling time?"* Same
   descriptive kind, but the answer must hold for a population you did not measure,
   paid for with a sampling design that makes your twelve stand in for the rest.
   Position: **Generalization**.
3. *"A new mug arrives tomorrow; how long until it cools to 60 °C?"* Still
   descriptive, because you only need a pattern that travels to a case you have not
   seen. Position: **Prediction**. You forecast the cooling time without knowing why
   any particular mug runs fast or slow.
4. *"Does adding a lid slow the cooling?"* Now the kind flips. To answer it you must
   compare the same mug in two worlds, one with a lid and one without, and only one of
   those worlds actually happens. That imagined comparison is the signature of a
   **causal** question. Position: **Causal reasoning**.

One move finishes the skill. A lab partner offers what they call one question: "how
fast do our mugs cool, and does a lid slow them down?" It is **double-barreled**,
fusing the descriptive headcount of question 1 with the causal comparison of question
4, and no single design answers both. You split it, and each half keeps its own
position.

## The Colab laboratory

This chapter's lab is notebook **nb01 — From curiosity to a research problem: descriptive, predictive, causal**. Open it in Colab with the
badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you walk your own
curiosity down to a question, watch a naive keyword sorter get fooled on a deck of
eight questions, and place your own question on the compass in the shape milestone M1
will grade.

## Recommended AI prompts

Commit your own answer first in every case. Write your classification down before you
open the tool, so its reply has something of yours to disagree with.

Classify, after you have classified it yourself:

```text
Act as a research-methods tutor. I have already classified my question:
[paste your question and your own kind + reach]. For my question, give its
KIND (descriptive or causal) and REACH (data at hand, a population, or
unseen cases), name the single word that most influenced you, and say
whether answering it needs an intervention or only a pattern. Use a table.
```

*After running, verify (counters **silent scope change**):* reread the position it
returns against the two definitions. If it calls your question causal, confirm your
design ever intervenes; if it only observes what already is, the honest kind is
descriptive.

Split a question you suspect is double-barreled:

```text
Here is a question I think secretly asks two things: [paste it]. Split it
into exactly two clean questions. For each half name its KIND and REACH. Do
not add any unit, outcome, or population that was not in my sentence.
```

*After running, verify (counters **illusion of completeness**):* a tidy split can
quietly drop a half or smuggle in a new variable. Confirm both halves together cover
your whole sentence and that neither added anything you did not write.

Red-team your own placement:

```text
Act as a hostile methodologist. Here is my question and the compass position
I assigned it: [paste both]. Argue the strongest case that I classified it
wrong. If you claim it is causal, name the method you would use and the one
assumption that method needs, in a table.
```

*After running, verify (counters **sycophantic agreement** and **plausible-but-wrong
method**):* praise with no objection is a red flag, so force the objection. For any
method it names, check whether your data can actually meet the assumption it lists.
An assumption you cannot meet turns a confident method into a wrong one.

::: {.callout-important title="Do not delegate"}
Three decisions stay yours: which problem you will study, how you word your question,
and which compass position it belongs to. A tool can offer a label, but only you can
decide what answer your question truly wants, and only you defend the placement at the
studio. If you cannot classify your question cleanly yet, that is a finding, not a
failure; a question that will not sit in one box is usually still double-barreled, and
naming that is progress.
:::

## An AI failure case

Suppose your project only records the cooling curves of bottles that are already
vacuum-insulated. Nothing is ever assigned; you measure bottles as they are. You ask a
chatbot to classify "is a vacuum-insulated bottle an effective heat barrier?" It sees
the word "effective," reads the question as causal, and confidently recommends a
randomized experiment that assigns bottles to insulation on or off. That is fluent and
well-formatted, and wrong for your design, because no un-insulated comparison world
exists anywhere in your data. The word "effective" is a **giveaway
word**, a surface hint the tool trusted instead of asking what answer the question
wants. You catch it the same way every time: reread the question against the two
definitions and ask whether your design ever intervenes. It does not, so the question
is descriptive (Description for your bottles, Generalization if it reaches to all
bottles of the model), and you reject the causal label as a silent scope change.

## Verification laboratory

The chapter's key result is a classification, which is a judgment call, so the fitting
check from the [verification guide](../../ai_resources/verification_guide.md) is
**peer reasoning**. Explain your placement to a classmate or the instructor, walking
them through the two definitions, and see whether the reasoning survives their hardest
question. Do it after you have committed your own placement, so their challenge has
something to push against. When your placement is causal, add a second, independent
check: draw a **causal diagram** of what causes what and confirm your design isolates
the effect, meaning a real comparison to a world that did not happen exists. Two
checks that agree beat one, and guard against the trap where a tool and its own
re-check share the same flaw.

## Project transfer

This chapter builds the spine of your milestone **M1, the research opportunity
landscape** you pitch at the Friday studio. Take the strongest curiosity from your
nb00 curiosity map, walk it down to a single question, and place that question on the
compass in the exact shape M1 grades: "my question is a [descriptive or causal]
inquiry reaching [the data at hand / a population / unseen cases], because the answer
it wants is [what]." Then name the one claim that position permits and the one it
forbids. Every design declaration you make for the rest of the term lives inside the
box you draw here.

## Defend your decision

In a short paragraph, state your project's question, naming its units of analysis, its
outcome, and its target population, then give its compass position in the M1 shape
above. Defend it: name the one claim the position permits and the boundary it draws
(the reach it does not reach, and whether it earns the word "because"), and close with
one sentence of honest uncertainty about the classification or the problem. A
placement with no stated boundary and no stated uncertainty is incomplete, however
confident it sounds.
